summary: I was born in the city of Niimiya, Wakayama, on March 10 in the 26th year of Meiji. It was a peaceful place during my childhood, and I spent my early years there before moving abroad. Niimiya holds my earliest memories and marks the beginning of my journey.

Niimiya, where I was born, is located in Wakayama Prefecture. Growing up in Wakayama, I experienced a calm and peaceful society. It was in this prefecture that my family, who were Christians, lived and where I completed my education before leaving Japan.

I was born during the Meiji period, specifically in its 26th year, which places my birth in the early 1890s. This period was a time of great change in Japan, but I personally have no memories of the earlier conflicts like the Sino-Japanese War. The Meiji era shaped the Japan I grew up in, peaceful and stable for my family.

I have no recollection of the Sino-Japanese War. It must have occurred before I was old enough to remember, so it did not directly affect my early life in Wakayama. My memories begin more clearly with later events like the Russo-Japanese War.

During the Russo-Japanese War, I remember the gay victory celebration vividly. It was a proud moment for Japan and for my community in Wakayama. That event left a strong impression on me as a child growing up during those times.

I have no impression of World War I because I was in Japan at the time and had no direct relationship with it. The war seemed distant from my daily life, and I was focused on my studies and family during those years.

I read about the Manchurian events in the newspapers while I was still in Japan. Though I did not experience them firsthand, these events were significant in the news and shaped the political atmosphere I observed. They were part of the broader tensions in East Asia at the time.

I came to the United States because my husband asked me to join him here. I arrived in San Francisco toward the end of July, 1921, on the ship Rakuyo Maru. I thought America was a cultural country and hoped to build a good home with my husband. Over time, I decided to stay permanently after my children started attending high school.

I arrived in San Francisco toward the end of July, 1921, after a difficult sea voyage where I suffered from seasickness. It was my first impression of America, and I found the streets beautiful. San Francisco was my entry point into a new life in the United States.

I traveled to America on the ship Rakuyo Maru, which was making its maiden voyage bound for South America. Most passengers were headed to South America, but I was going to the United States to join my husband. The journey was difficult, especially because of my seasickness.

Although the Rakuyo Maru was bound for South America and most passengers were going there, I was traveling to the United States to join my husband. South America was part of the ship's route, but it was not my destination or focus during that journey.

I lived in Utah during the time of the war between Japan and America, and because of that, I was not relocated. Living in Utah provided some stability during a difficult period when attitudes toward Japanese were cold. It was where I raised my children and decided to stay permanently.

My family was Christian, and I hold dear the passage from the Lord's Prayer: 'Thy will be done on earth as it is in heaven.' I hope that churches can enlighten society and bring people together. This prayer has been a source of comfort and guidance throughout my life.
